speaker: interviewer
text: In London, England, this is tape 2. You told me something about your, your early childhood and some of the anti-Semitism that you experienced. And when were you first aware of when the war started? What sort of impact did that make?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, that was-- I'll start from here. On the 31st of August, 1939, my father put my mother and the two of us-- two children-- on a train going from Cracow to Lwow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was-- the war was very imminent. It was hanging in the air by that time. Everybody knew it. And there was quite a bit of panic around and so forth. But, to be quite honest, I don't think anybody expected it just there and then. It was hanging, suspended in the air. We were all aware. Everybody was sort of preparing for it, but that it will happen the following day-- we, anyway the civilian population hardly knew. And so we put us on the train. He, himself, stayed behind because, as he said, oh, he has to arrange a few more things. And I'll join you in a day or two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why to, to Lwow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my grandparents lived there and because whoever could was trying to get away, as far as possible, from the Germans. One expected that they would occupy. And if so, one wanted to be as far as possible away from them. And so we went east. And we were in this particularly lucky position, if you will, that, that we had where to go, to whom to go to. Many people didn't-- just went to the unknown, absolutely. We went to my mother's parents, my grandparents.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he put us on the train, and we went on that train every year before, as I said. We went for Pesach. And it was always a great adventure for us children to go on the train. It's not like today. But in those days, it was quite a journey, you know. It was enterprising to go on a train, a nice train, was it first class or seated comfortably and so forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: And every time we went, every year, my mother's family lived all over, sort of outside of Lwow. And they would come to the main station. Would be, say, a station called [INAUDIBLE]. It's-- it's a well-known town in Poland. And the train, one would stop there. I mean, at least 15 people would wait for us, to see us, to receive us, to-- bringing food parcels, you name it, because the poor children may be hungry. Oh, you know, that sort of thing-- so it was always such a-- something to look forward to, they one hand, we on the other hand, and particularly, we, the children. And they brought yummies and goodies, and you know. And then the next station, again, some family would wait. My mother had a very extended family. She didn't have any brothers and sisters, but she was one of nine children. And she was the only one to survive past the age of one month. They all-- she was the first one and the only one to really live and survive. The others died, god knows what. They blue-- blue babies or what. Nobody ever knew. So you can imagine the apple of the parents' eye-- I mean, one child out of nine. And so anyway, there we were. But this occasion on the 31st of August, 1939, that particular train was stopping at every, every village, not just every town, every little village. The journey took 12 hours or, or close to 12 hours, whereas normally it would take four hours or five. I can't remember exactly, but it took a whole day. We arrived in Lwow at about 10:00 in the evening. And as it was still summery, and so it wasn't exactly dark, it was sort of semi. And a most incredible sight to behold, for me, was, like for everybody else on that train, I imagine-- the entire platform was full of soldiers in uniforms-- all dressed, helmets, everything-- lying down on the platform sleeping, with their gear and everything next to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you know, like sardines, one next to another-- it was, it was such an incredible sight. I mean, we-- I can't describe this to you. And we, we thought, how are we going to get out of the train, not to step on the soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there they were, poor things, and they were all asleep. We went out. My grandfather was waiting for us, took us home, and so on. The following morning, very early, must have been 5:00, 6:00 in the morning, the Germans bombed Lwow. And the bomb-- the, the places they bombed, the first one was the station, railway station. And all these soldiers were killed. So that is such a poignant, terrible thing. I mean, I see it so vividly. At least this is what we were told, that all the soldiers being-- who had been waiting there for the-- for being, I don't know, to be-- to be transported to whatever divisions or whatever they were going-- wherever they were going. All of them were killed by this bomb. And that was ready terrible, and that's where the war started. And immediately we were being bombed. And of course, no one was, as I said, quite prepared that it was there and then already. We were there. My father was not. We were completely cut off. He stayed behind. We didn't know what happened. We had no way of establishing what happened to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: In a very short time, the Russians, the Russians came in and occupied our part, this part of Poland, precisely until [INAUDIBLE], they divided Poland between them, Russia and Germany, as you know. And, and we were on the Russian side. My father was on the other side. There was no communication whatsoever. We didn't know-- is he dead? Is he alive, where, what? So it was rather a difficult time, particularly for my mother and-- well, for all of us, naturally. When we almost gave up every hope that we should ever see him again, one day, in walked-- walks a man who could have been-- what can I say, like, like Robinson Crusoe, you know, with a beard up to here, black. He was-- he was dark like I am, a black beard, wild eyes, torn in shreds, clothing, absolutely, absolutely, a horrifying sight. And he was just not the same man. And it was very difficult to establish from him, there and then, what transpired, how, where. How did he get here? But later on we established that, of course, he was taken into the army. And then slowly, slowly, as the Polish army this-- what is the word? That they went into disarray.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everybody was told to save himself the best they know how. And so he walked through a part of Poland by foot, you know, from a place called Kielce. This is where they were fighting. He walked by foot to Lwow. And the Germans were all over the place, and it was very difficult to escape. But somehow or other, he managed and he came. And of course, he was quite ill for quite some time. I mean, he-- nightmares, nightmares, shouting, screaming at night-- the horrible things he saw. And, and that was all very, very, very awful, to say-- to say the least.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this? [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewer
text: But when in '39 did he get to you, and how long did it take to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it would have been October, around end of September, beginning of October, around that time. And then he spent quite some time in the hospital because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they said the doctors presumed that it was a nervous condition. My father was a sportsman. He was a very good-looking man, very sporty, every sport-- skiing, tennis, football, everything. He was full of life, very vivacious, very, very-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, a lively person. Suddenly, there is a man who is totally, totally broken. His spirit was broken. That's what it was. It wasn't purely physical. And he was-- he actually-- there was a moment where he was almost paralyzed. He could not move. Doctors didn't know, and then they assumed that it is a nervous condition, purely out of nerves. He had the sciatic nerve effected. And in those days, it was rather difficult to treat it. There weren't all sorts of medications that we have today. And-- sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's all right. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Let's just [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I'm still recording.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, so are we recording, yes? I'm sorry. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so they were treating him sort of very conventionally. And, and it took a very, very long time for him. And then he was walking with a stick. For us it was a terrible sight to behold, because there was this young man, and suddenly he was an old man. And he couldn't bear the thought that he was with the Russians. He absolutely disliked the Russians. He, he didn't trust them from here to there. And so they were all psychological aspects to it. And anyway, there we were. And it was not easy. We were in occupied territory. We had to now try and find some kind of accommodation, because my grandparents-- I mean, they, they lived, but they didn't have an enormous accommodation, and we were four people extra.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my parents found something, eventually, in the center of town. And we had, which was almost palatial in those days, we had two rooms and a kitchen. Of course, no, no such luxury as a toilet. That was all outside. You had to walk outside. The water was outside, everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, but we were together. And we had two rooms and a kitchenette, and so on. My mother was sewing and making a living by sewing. My father had to go into work because otherwise that was-- so he couldn't work in his capacity. That was certainly not [LAUGHS] what they needed. But he was very, very-- apart from the fact, what I said yesterday, that he was sort of jolly, and, and full of fun, and, and sporty and so-- he was very industrious. He-- there wasn't such a thing that he didn't know how to do with his hands. He, he had wonderful hands. And he could do things, build things, repair things. Everything, he could do with his hands. And so he put himself up as a, a cabinet maker, carpenter, cabinet maker, and so on. And he could. He could do anything, and he did beautiful things. So they employed him as such in the Russian institution. And he worked as such, and all was well. And in fact, they liked him very much. Primarily, they liked his work, because his work was not simply just, just a little table or something. It was done. It was finished. He thought about what it. It was done intelligently, put it this way, maybe.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that made the difference between just an ordinary carpenter and him. And they were always sort of come and looking. How is it-- how is he doing this [INAUDIBLE] smoothly, so nicely? And they liked him. And they forever sent some things for the children, the home, via him.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian officers were, you know, very nice to him. But he didn't trust them. At one point, not very long after [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the occupation, they put out notices all over town that anybody who wishes to go back to their homes on the other side of the river San, which is to the German-occupied part of Poland, is welcome to do so. If they have families there, if they left families there, if they're here on their own, they are welcome to do so. Would they please come and register on such-and-such a day, and they can go back. Well, there were a lot of, particularly, men who were on their own, because they were taken to the army. When everything was dismantled and, you know, disarray, they, like my father, came, escaped on their own. And there they were. The whole family would have stayed behind, not like we. We were unique in, in the sense that we were a unit, a family unit on this side. They usually-- the man went to the army. The family stayed behind. And a lot of them were coming to us. They were friends from Cracow, and so they were coming. Our home was always full of these people who were on their own. So my mother and my father, oh, they're on their own. Of course, they have to come and eat with us. They have to, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when this-- when these notices came up, they were over the moon. Wonderful, we can now go back to the family, which is understandable, right? My father said, I'm not registering. Ah, you're crazy. Why do you want to stay here? You hate them, and so why don't-- he said, yes. But I hate the Germans even more. Why should I want to? I'm no register-- I don't trust their notices. I tell you, don't register.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they all went and registered. And in one night, one night only, all these people were taken away and sent to Russia, and we were not. We stayed behind. Of course, everybody was saying, my god. This is a clever man. Look at him, he-- he knew he couldn't trust these Russians. And he didn't register, and all of those-- and you know, that was the, the consensus of opinion. How brilliant, on his part, not to register. In the meantime, as it transpired, we would have wished, later on, that he would have registered, and that we would, all of us, have been taken away to Russia, rather than to undergo what we underwent later on. But it so happened. And so as we stayed behind, he was working. My mother was sewing. And so they made their bread and butter somehow, maybe not so much butter, but certainly a bit of bread and a soup. My grandparents were helping all the time, of course. And here we were. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When the situation became rather dangerous again, toward the-- toward June of 1941, and one thought and saw that things are not very good, and it's very possible that the Russians may withdraw, and the Germans may, indeed, occupy us, the Russians who were going back-- some of the people, for whom my father was working, said, you must come with us. And he said no. God knows why he said no. He just had such a-- such a-- such a feeling against these Russians. He said no. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one officer came with a lorry to our home, to our house, in front of the house, brought the lorry, came in and said, here is a lorry. I want you to put your wife, your children, and whatever belongings you can onto this lorry, and we'll drive you to Russia. And he said no. And the officer said, you will regret it. You will regret it. He said no, and we stayed behind. And in June, 1941, we were occupied by the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I just ask what you and your brother did up until that time? You said about your father doing carpentry and your mother--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my brother was a little-- he was a baby. He was a boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were little.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, sure, and so was I. There were schools that were open, and so I went to a school, which was fine. In fact, may I have a drink of water? Sorry. [DRINKS] In fact, I have a little-- I had a little bit of singing voice or something. And the Russians, as you know, the moment-- sorry-- the moment they see a bit of talent anywhere, they-- or they did, in those days anyway-- they do help it along. And so I sang in the school choir, in the chorus. Then I went to the conservatoire in Lwow and studied the piano and also chorus singing and so on. Such And then they were always getting me out of the chorus and putting me in-- put in the front. And I had a little bit of a solo part, and so on. So it was all for fun. It was fun. I loved it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then one day, around beginning of June, end of May, 1941, they came up to my parents and said that they would like to send me-- the whole choir is going to Kiev, Moscow. They would like to send me as a soloist with, with this choir. And would the parents have anything against it? I mean, they have to ask and so forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they will see, because they might even try and keep me there, and you know, to, to go to the conservatoire, that the music school, and study the voice. And my father said, well, yes, of course. Yes, she may go. But of course, it never happened, because, again, the Russian-German war broke out. The pact was broken between these wonderful two [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] nations, and, and the Germans occupied us. My little brother was in a kindergarten. He kept reading all sorts of incredible books. We slept in one bed, he and I. My parents slept in another bed. And that's how we lived. And he always had, under his pillow, some amazing books on geology, on mineralogy, on-- and nobody knew where he got them from. He brought them from somewhere. You know, children were playing together, and so. And some people are always inviting, because he was such a young gentleman. He was a very gentle, very beautifully mannered young boy. People loved him. Everyone loved him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so they asked him to their homes, and he would see selling something. May I borrow this? So they, they laughed and said, yes, of course. [INAUDIBLE] you know, laughingly. He would bring, put it under his pillow, and he would read these books. And he was very frail, very sort of slim. And, and in the kindergarten they said to my mother, please, will you remove absolutely everything from sight, from that child? He is not allowed to read, or to do, or to play chess the way he does, because this child, you know, will never develop physically. He is so mentally over, over the top, over his, you know, his age limit. And-- but there was no way. He always found a way. People would always come, some people from up upstairs-- you know, grown-ups-- and ask, excuse me, may we borrow young Uri? I'd like to have a game of chess. My mother said, with my little boy? You want to have a game of chess? Yes, oh, he's a wonderful player. Didn't know-- that's during the Russian occupation.

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What else can I tell you? When the Germans came in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of course, at the very first-- I mean, to start with, everyone who was in a state of shock.

speaker: interviewee
text: One didn't know what to expect, what is awaiting, because of course, nobody knew. We couldn't presume for a moment, because that was just-- that was beyond one's, one's fantasy. But from day to day, we didn't know. What we did know was that the letters from the people from the other side, in other words, the occupied Poland, by the Germans, before-- those letters seemed quite good. In those days, people were still working in their own capacities. They lived in their flats or apartments, if you like. They, they were not really handicapped very badly. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: They had food. There was rationing, but not too bad. In other words, one didn't know-- what is it all about? Why is it that they're talking such terrible things about these Germans? Not so bad at all-- they're letting us live. They're letting us live quite decently.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what we heard. On the other hand, from Russia, the letters that were coming from Russia were horrors, horror stories. People were starving. People were in Siberia in terrible, terrible conditions and terrible climatic conditions. They needed food. They needed this, they needed-- they were not dressed adequately. And we were sending parcels to Russia, while we were occupied by the Russians. On the other side, in Germany, everything was fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when the Germans came, we didn't know what to expect. And we were hoping that, hopefully, we should somehow be able to, to survive that, however long this occupation will take.

speaker: interviewee
text: But slowly, slowly, things were not so good. Of course, what I did not mention-- maybe I should have mentioned that when the Russians came-- I'll go back for a moment. When the Russians came in, immediately, almost immediately, they disposed with practically the entire intelligentsia in Lwow-- professorships, you know; professors; doctors; of course, officers. Professional officers were taken away and killed, were taken to prisons, tortured, but mostly killed. The intelligentsia went immediately. They disposed of it, exactly as the Germans did with-- when they came. That was the first thing they did, disposed with the hierarchy of the-- not only Jewish, also Polish, also Polish, not only Jewish. And so that was, you know-- when one saw and heard all these incredible scenes, which happened immediately, and so one knew what one was to, to expect, what was to come. [DRINKS]
